examples.title: FCFS (First Come First Serve) Disk Scheduling
examples.description: Implements the basic FCFS algorithm that services disk requests in the order they arrive, regardless of their position on the disk.
examples.code:
# FCFS Disk Scheduling Algorithm
# Input: Current head position and list of disk requests

def fcfs(head, requests):
    """
    FCFS: Serves requests in the order they arrive.
    """
    seek_count = 0
    current_position = head
    sequence = []
    
    print(f"Initial Head Position: {head}")
    print(f"Requests: {requests}")
    print("-" * 40)
    
    for request in requests:
        distance = abs(request - current_position)
        seek_count += distance
        sequence.append((current_position, request, distance))
        print(f"Move from {current_position} to {request}: Seek distance = {distance}")
        current_position = request
    
    print("-" * 40)
    print(f"Total Seek Time: {seek_count}")
    print(f"Average Seek Time: {seek_count / len(requests):.2f}")
    print(f"Seek Sequence: {[head] + requests}")
    
    return seek_count, sequence

# Example usage
head_position = 50
disk_requests = [82, 170, 43, 140, 24, 16, 190]
fcfs(head_position, disk_requests)
examples.explanation:
1. Start at the initial head position (50)
2. For each request in the order received: calculate absolute distance from current position, add to total seek time, move head to that position
3. FCFS is simple but not optimal - it may cause excessive head movement
4. Total seek time = sum of all individual seek distances

examples.title: SSTF (Shortest Seek Time First) Disk Scheduling
examples.description: Implements SSTF algorithm that always selects the request closest to the current head position, reducing average seek time compared to FCFS.
examples.code:
# SSTF (Shortest Seek Time First) Disk Scheduling Algorithm

def sstf(head, requests):
    """
    SSTF: Always selects the nearest request to the current head position.
    """
    seek_count = 0
    current_position = head
    remaining_requests = requests.copy()
    sequence = []
    
    print(f"Initial Head Position: {head}")
    print(f"Requests: {requests}")
    print("-" * 40)
    
    while remaining_requests:
        # Find the request with minimum distance from current position
        closest = min(remaining_requests, key=lambda x: abs(x - current_position))
        distance = abs(closest - current_position)
        seek_count += distance
        sequence.append((current_position, closest, distance))
        print(f"Move from {current_position} to {closest}: Seek distance = {distance}")
        
        current_position = closest
        remaining_requests.remove(closest)
    
    print("-" * 40)
    print(f"Total Seek Time: {seek_count}")
    print(f"Average Seek Time: {seek_count / len(requests):.2f}")
    
    return seek_count, sequence

# Example usage
head_position = 50
disk_requests = [82, 170, 43, 140, 24, 16, 190]
result = sstf(head_position, disk_requests)
examples.explanation:
1. Start at initial head position
2. At each step, find the request closest to current head (minimum absolute difference)
3. Service that request and move head there
4. Repeat until all requests are served
5. SSTF reduces average seek time but may cause starvation for distant requests

examples.title: SCAN (Elevator) Disk Scheduling Algorithm
examples.description: Implements the SCAN algorithm (also known as Elevator algorithm) where the head moves in one direction servicing all requests until it reaches the end, then reverses direction.
examples.code:
# SCAN (Elevator) Disk Scheduling Algorithm

def scan(head, requests, disk_size=200):
    """
    SCAN: Head moves in one direction servicing all requests,
    then reverses direction at the end.
    """
    seek_count = 0
    current_position = head
    sequence = []
    
    # Sort requests and separate into left and right of head
    left = [r for r in requests if r < head]
    right = [r for r in requests if r >= head]
    
    # Sort both lists
    left.sort(reverse=True)  # Process far left first
    right.sort()  # Process near right first
    
    print(f"Initial Head Position: {head}")
    print(f"Requests: {requests}")
    print(f"Disk Size: {disk_size}")
    print("-" * 40)
    
    # First, go to the right end
    # Process all requests on the right side
    for request in right:
        distance = abs(request - current_position)
        seek_count += distance
        sequence.append((current_position, request, distance))
        print(f"Move from {current_position} to {request}: Seek distance = {distance}")
        current_position = request
    
    # Go to the end of disk
    if right:
        distance = abs(disk_size - 1 - current_position)
        seek_count += distance
        print(f"Move to disk end {disk_size - 1}: Seek distance = {distance}")
        current_position = disk_size - 1
    
    # Now reverse and go left
    for request in left:
        distance = abs(request - current_position)
        seek_count += distance
        sequence.append((current_position, request, distance))
        print(f"Move from {current_position} to {request}: Seek distance = {distance}")
        current_position = request
    
    print("-" * 40)
    print(f"Total Seek Time: {seek_count}")
    print(f"Average Seek Time: {seek_count / len(requests):.2f}")
    
    return seek_count, sequence

# Example usage
head_position = 50
disk_requests = [82, 170, 43, 140, 24, 16, 190]
scan(head_position, disk_requests, disk_size=200)
examples.explanation:
1. Sort requests into those less than head (left) and greater/equal (right)
2. Move head right, servicing all requests in increasing order
3. When no more requests on right, go to disk end (200)
4. Reverse direction and service remaining (left) requests in decreasing order
5. SCAN provides better response time and avoids starvation
